turns.content: I'm working through the Rust book exercises and I keep getting confused about what's the difference between using a tagged enum versus an untagged enum when deserializing from multiple sources like JSON or YAML. Can you explain how serde handles this?

The key distinction is that a tagged enum requires explicit field names in the input data to identify which variant it represents, whereas an untagged enum lets serde infer the variant based on the structure itself. When you use serde with the Derive macro and set the attribute to derive(Serialize) and Deserialize(untagged), the library attempts to match fields against your variants without needing a discriminator field. This is useful when you're deserializing from multiple sources like JSON or YAML where the format might vary.

Wait, so if I have an untagged enum with three variants, and the input data only matches one of them, will serde just pick that one automatically? That sounds too good to be true for a production system.

That's exactly how it works in theory, but there's a catch you need to watch out for. If the input data doesn't match any of your variants, serde will panic by default unless you configure fallback behavior explicitly. You can use the From trait or custom deserialization logic to handle errors gracefully instead of crashing. This is critical when dealing with multiple sources like JSON and YAML where one source might be malformed.
Oh, I see. So if I'm using serde_json::from_str on a string that doesn't match my enum, it will just fail hard unless I add some error handling? That seems fragile for a real-world API.

You're right to be concerned about fragility. The standard serde approach won't automatically fall back silently; you need to implement custom logic using the From trait or wrap your deserialization in a try block and handle the Result types manually. This is why advanced systems often combine untagged enums with fallback strategies that attempt multiple formats before giving up.

I'm trying to deserialize JSON into a Rust struct using serde but I keep hitting errors when the input format changes slightly. What's the difference between untagged enums and fallback deserialization in serde_json?

Untagged enums let you try multiple formats by matching against a set of possible variants without requiring a tag field, while fallback deserialization lets you attempt one format first and then automatically retry another if that fails. For example, you can define an enum with variants like ConfigV1 and ConfigV2, then use serde_json::from_str on the untagged version so it tries to match against both formats in sequence.
That sounds too abstract. I just want something concrete where I can actually parse a config file that might be in V1 or V2 format without writing custom error handling logic every time.

You can wrap your untagged enum in a macro called serde::de::untagged to create a flexible deserializer. When you serialize the struct with this macro, it automatically tries each variant until one matches without requiring explicit tagging or manual error checking.
Okay so I just need to add the untagged macro and then serde_json can handle both formats? But what if neither format matches?

If neither variant matches, you'll get a deserialization error from serde_json that tells you which variants were tried. You can catch this with Result<_, Box<dyn Error>> and then fall back to your own custom parser if needed.

I need to parse a config file that might come in JSON or TOML format, but I thought if I just used serde_json::from_str on the string it would automatically handle both formats without me needing to write any custom logic.

That won't work because serde_json::from_str only understands strict JSON syntax and will panic immediately if it encounters a TOML token. You need the serde::Deserialize trait combined with an untagged enum so the deserializer can try one format, fail gracefully, and then retry another. Think of it like having two different keys for a single lock; you don't just use one key hoping it opens everything—you must have a mechanism to switch between them automatically.
Okay, so I should define an enum with variants representing the JSON structure and the TOML structure, right? And then use serde::DeserializeOn from_str on that enum?

Almost correct; you define the enum with variants matching each format's shape, but you must mark it as untagged using #[serde(untagged)] so the deserializer doesn't require a discriminator field. Then you wrap your data structure inside those variants and use serde_json::from_str or serde_yaml::from_str depending on which parser succeeds first before falling back to the other.
Wait, I'm confused about how exactly it tries formats without me writing an explicit if-else loop? Does serde handle the branching internally?

Yes, serde handles all the branching internally once you declare the untagged enum; you only provide the variants and the trait bound. The deserializer will attempt to match incoming bytes against each variant in order, skipping over mismatched syntax automatically until it finds a successful parse. It's like having multiple doors labeled differently but sharing one entryway—the system just tries opening them all until one clicks.

So if I define an enum with JSON and TOML variants marked as untagged, then put my config struct inside those variants, serde will automatically attempt to deserialize the input string against each variant without me writing conditional logic?

Exactly; that's the core advantage of this pattern. Once you set up the untagged enum with your structs nested inside the variants, the deserializer handles all the format detection and fallback logic for you. You can then wrap the result in a trait like Result<T, E> to catch failures cleanly without needing manual error handling loops.
